stem: Which quotation from the passage best supports the claim that multitasking reduces work efficiency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: "people can't multitask very well, and when people say they can, they're deluding themselves"

choices.label: B
choices.text: "participants who switched between tasks took up to 25% longer to complete their work compared to those who focused on one task at a time"

choices.label: C
choices.text: "when we think we're multitasking, we're actually rapidly switching our attention between tasks"

choices.label: D
choices.text: "focusing on one task at a time remains the most effective approach to productivity"

explanation: Choice B provides the most direct and specific evidence supporting the claim that multitasking reduces work efficiency. The quotation presents concrete experimental data showing that task-switching resulted in a measurable 25% increase in completion time compared to single-task focus. This quantitative evidence directly demonstrates reduced efficiency, making it the strongest support for the claim about multitasking's negative impact on productivity.

distractorRationale.A: While this quote establishes that multitasking ability is often overestimated, it doesn't provide specific evidence about efficiency or productivity outcomes.
distractorRationale.B: This is the correct answer as it provides concrete quantitative evidence of reduced efficiency.
distractorRationale.C: This quote explains the mechanism of multitasking (rapid attention switching) but doesn't directly demonstrate reduced efficiency.
distractorRationale.D: This quote states a conclusion about single-tasking being more effective but doesn't provide the supporting evidence for that claim.

passage: The widespread belief that multitasking enhances productivity has been thoroughly debunked by cognitive research. Dr. Earl Miller, a neuroscientist at MIT, explains that "people can't multitask very well, and when people say they can, they're deluding themselves. The brain is very good at deluding itself." Studies consistently show that attempting to perform multiple tasks simultaneously actually decreases overall efficiency. In one landmark experiment, participants who switched between tasks took up to 25% longer to complete their work compared to those who focused on one task at a time. Furthermore, the quality of work suffered significantly during multitasking conditions. Brain imaging studies reveal that when we think we're multitasking, we're actually rapidly switching our attention between tasks, a process that creates mental fatigue and increases the likelihood of errors. As cognitive psychologist Dr. David Meyer notes, "Even brief mental blocks created by shifting between tasks can cost as much as 25% of the time needed to complete a project." The evidence is clear: despite our intuitions, focusing on one task at a time remains the most effective approach to productivity.
